sentences.text: This is Mike, my youngest son, and I'm very proud of him.
He's here with me in Florida.
And perhaps he wants to say a few words.
MIKE (SON): Yes.
It's made me very proud that my father has had this opportunity to share with you his experiences during the-- during the war.

My brother and mother and I have, along with the rest of our family, have supported him through the last eight or nine years worth of his research and compiling all the information that he was able to share with you.
And do you-- have you learned a great deal from all this?

MIKE (SON): Yes, I believe-- I have learned a great deal from the information that my father has, and-- and will be able to give that information on to other people, as well as learn from the experiences in my current life.
Thanks Mike.
Thank you very much.
Robert, would you say your full name for us please?
My name is Robert Richard Weiss.
W-E-I-S-S.
I was born March 16, 1923 in Vienna, Austria.
I am 72 and 1/2.
My parents was Richard and Josephina Weiss.
We had a very large family in Vienna.
For example, my father had nine brothers and sisters, and my mother eight.
And we had countless cousins.
And we were-- we were a very close family, which means we met frequently.
On all Jewish holidays we met and we joined together.
My father was an college grad, was an officer in the Imperial Austrian Army in the First World War.
He loved music, the arts.
He had a large library at home, and I look always up to him for his guidance.
And he was, for example, an philatelist, and I'm still today a philatelist, because I learned that from him.
He was the owner of an textile mail order business in Vienna, which he ran with my-- with my mother.
And it was also under the name of Richard, [?
Rickert ?] and Josephina Weiss.
It was located in the Ninth District.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The Ninth District had also an-- a temple, and I believe it was the Mllnergasse.
Unfortunately, this temple doesn't exist anymore.
It was destroyed on the-- during the Crystal Night fires, where all the temples of Vienna were burned and destroyed.
Only one survived, which exists still today.

The reason of the survival of this particular temple is because it was in the house of the Jewish community, and the Nazis wanted to preserve this particular building, because all the archives and all the activities were done there.
So that's the reason, actually, this temple survived and exists today.
I had a very happy childhood.
And my childhood ended on March 13, 1938, three days before my 15th birthday.
That was the entry of the Germans and the occupation of Austria.
Hitler came to Austria, and, of course, our life changed.
Yes.
I have an older brother by the name of Victor.
He's two years older than me.
He went in Vienna to the gimnazjum.
And he-- he was fortunate to finish it.

My-- my childhood ended because on this particular day, we saw an-- we saw the Viennese population out in the streets greeting German troops and German officials in a manner we never saw, we never thought possible before.
And that was also the first time I heard shouting anti-Semitic remarks, which I actually didn't hear before.
Like, [NON-ENGLISH] Jews, right.
The Horst-Wessel-Lied, the song of Horst-Wessel was a song, and one sentence is till the Jews' blood drips from our knives.
And that an-- that was a shock for me, because I went to a public school.
I would say all, or most of my friends, were not Jewish.
In my particular class, we were only two Jews.
All other children were Gentiles.
Our Jewish [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] education was once or twice a week for an hour or two.
The Jewish children in this particular school, which is called Renngasse Hauptschule were brought together into one class room.
And an rabbi from the Jewish community came and gave us religious [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] introduction.
And I went only very infrequently to the temple, although I had bar mitzvah.
And also my grandparents observed all the Jewish holidays.
But my father and my close family was not really, you know, by no means orthodox.
We were more what was called reformed Jews.
No.
They-- they didn't.
Austria is to the largest extent an Catholic country.
And that is also the national religion, and all other religions were small minorities.
I believe in-- in Vienna, by March 1938, there were only perhaps 200,000 Jews living.
And-- and the rest were Catholic.
Right.
Most of them were Catholic.
Right.
Well, I really don't recall that I ever experienced antisemitism.
But now that you mention that, I remember there were a few German Jewish refugees coming to my father before the 1938 invasion of Austria, and they came from Germany.
And they told us many stories, which neither he or I or everybody else really believed them.
That was stories of beginning atrocities of Jews sent to detaining camps.
They were really not called concentration camps at that time, but later on we found that all-- all those stories were true.
Ah, well, very slowly we started to experience difficulties.
In-- before the-- the school really ended, I was already expelled.
One day, during the classes, the director of the school came into the classroom and said, all the Jewish children stand up and leave.
We don't want you anymore here.
Just like that.
And that was the end of my public education.
The basis for him was that a directive came to have his school Judenfrei, which means no Jews could go into-- into the public school in Vienna.
They had to go to an Jewish school, which was set up immediately thereafter, or perhaps a couple of weeks later, from the Jewish Israelic cultures commander.
That's correct.
That was for the high schools as well as for the lower schools.
So all of a sudden, in one day, the Jewish children who were in public school could not continue.
Were expelled.
Well, of course, surprise.
On the other hand, they also expected something to happen, because around the same time, signs were placed in the public gardens.
Jews not allowed to sit on this particular bench.
The movie theaters had signs, Jews not allowed.
You could go in no theater.
No public library would lend you a book.
They didn't want Jews to contaminate their books.
The-- the-- the population changed.
The-- how should I say, we heard many bad things thrown at us.
And also, the Jews started to leave Austria.
People which had means and people which had passports, because not everybody had an [INAUDIBLE] passport at that time.
A-- an-- an travel document.
Passport.
Left to Italy, to Hungary, to Czechoslovakia, wherever they could go.
Switzerland.
The more mobile people, I would say, started to leave.
Interesting enough, this question came up many times in my mind.
And I can only tell you certain names are strictly Jewish, or very, very much Jewish.
The Austrian, just by looking at you, could also, in many times, tell if you are Jewish or not.
And I think that was the beginning of the real antisemitism which was prevailing always in Austria.
I-- I don't think--
Or sit on a park bench.
I-- I don't think so.

I think that in the moment an directive came out and a sign was put up, the Jews were also afraid to go to a library or to cross the line and go into a movie theater when there was a-- a sign Jews were not allowed.

Because at the same time, people started to get picked up and thrown into camps, because they had previous political affiliations or they were outspoken, or they had professions which the Germans didn't like, like an reporter and newscaster.
I am talking about Jewish newscasters.
Yes, I have a-- a cousin by the name of Otto Perl.
O-T-T-O-P-E-R-L, which was, I think, four or five years older than me.
And in his Jews, he joined the [NON-ENGLISH] which was an-- an socialistic youth group, not because he was a communist, just because his friends were in this particular group.
When the German came to Austria, all socialistic groups were immediately prosecuted.
And, for example, my father was a Social Democrat.
In the United States, there are only Republicans-- only two.
But in Austria, there were many parties, and that was one of them.
And a lot of the leaders of this particular party were immediately imprisoned.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I mentioned my father.
My father was picked up on the street of Vienna on one day.
The-- the Austrian police had Nazi arm stripes and were under the control, at that time, already under-- from-- from German Nazis.
Closed up a street on both sides and took everyone which has-- which had Jewish in their [NON-ENGLISH]-- in the [NON-ENGLISH] cards.
Had Jewish papers.
You-- you see, in Austria, everybody had to have an-- when you go here to a bank, they ask you for your driver's license as a-- a-- a way of identifying yourself.
But in Austria, you had also cards.
And in a moment they saw that you are a Jew, they-- they threw you in jail.
Matter of fact, they were marked, [NON-ENGLISH], Jewish, [NON-ENGLISH].
So on a driver's license or identity card.
It was not a driver's license, it was identity cards, yes.
And an interesting part is that at that time, it was possible to get people out of prison and out of the concentration camps.
If you could show that they had an affidavit, a [NON-ENGLISH], and a-- and visa to emigrate immediately to out of Austria.
And, for example, my cousin Otto Perl, his parents got him an entry permit to England.
And after two, three months, he was released from Dachau.
And I remember seeing him without any hair, with-- with a view out of his eyes of an person which went through a tremendous difficult time.
And he had to immediately leave Austria.
My mother was able to get my father free.

The mail order business of my mother and father was taken over, what is-- what was called at the time, [NON-ENGLISH] taken away from the rightful owner and it was taken by two Austrian Nazis, one by the name of Krebs.
K-R-E-B-S.
And the second one by the name of Magyear, M-A-G-Y-E-A-R.
They took over the-- the business.
Of course not.
They came one day, and they say, well, a Jew is not allowed to have a mail order business.
We will run it.
That was a government proscription
Right.
And my father had to work for them without any salary for, I believe, three to four weeks to show them the-- the business.
Our employees just were taken over.
The stock and the-- the equipment, office equipment and so on were just taken.
At the same time, we had to leave our apartment too.
The building we were living in, 14 Porzellangasse in the Ninth-- Ninth District of Vienna, was declared Judenrein, which means the owner didn't want to have any Jews renting apartments.
So he wanted his building free of Jews, and that was very possible at that time.
And the Jews living there could only take along the things they could carry.
They had to leave all their furniture, all their belongings where they were.
And we moved in-- I'm speaking about my father and my mother and brother and me-- into an-- into an apartment owned by another Jewish family, which had, for example, an apartment of four rooms.
And we moved into two rooms of-- of four.

I think that my mother could free my father because she could prove that he was still needed in the business ran now by German owners or something, you know, that nature, which was an [NON-ENGLISH] business.
And my father came home.
I don't want to say an broken man, but he was changed in the few days, or perhaps, week, week and a half in-- in prison.
The-- the-- the building is called Rossauer Kaserne in the Vienna Ninth District.
They must have interrogated him in an very brutal fashion.
He never really spoke about that.
But physically, we saw there was a change, a-- physically as well as mentally.
We, of course, tried also to leave Austria.
Because-- because there are-- you know, we saw there was no future in us, for us.
Well, I have to go back a little bit.
In the First World War, my father had an officer's servant, which was an Austrian soldier by the name of Bauer, B-A-U-E-R.
He came of the part of Tyrol.
And after the War in 1920 or '22, the economic situation was very, very bad in Austria, and he tried to emigrate, leave Austria for Paraguay.
